metadata.working_title: ENACT: End-to-End Analysis of Visium High Definition Spatial Transcriptomics Data via Cell Segmentation and Bin-to-Cell Assignment

abstract_sketch.problem: Sequencing-based spatial transcriptomics has historically lacked single-cell resolution; Visium HD provides 2 micron by 2 micron bins but aggregating them into 8 micron by 8 micron spots does not cleanly map to individual cells.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing methods that combine imaging and sequencing (e.g. Bin2cell with Stardist-based outlines) work well for isolated cells but degrade when cells are tightly packed because a single bin can overlap multiple cells.
abstract_sketch.approach: We developed ENACT, a tissue-agnostic end-to-end pipeline that couples deep-learning cell segmentation (Stardist) with four bin-to-cell assignment strategies (a Naive method and three weighted methods) and three downstream cell-type annotation engines (Sargent, CellAssign, CellTypist), and exports SquidPy-compatible AnnData objects for spatial analysis.
abstract_sketch.key_results: On a Xenium-derived synthetic dataset with whole-cell boundaries, 25% of bins overlap more than one cell, causing the Naive method to lose recall; the Weighted-by-Area strategy achieves the highest F1-score. On a seqFISH+-derived synthetic dataset only ~5% of bins overlap more than one cell and all four strategies achieve precision close to 1 and recall close to 0.99. On 20,991 pathologist-annotated cells, Sargent combined with Weighted-by-Area yields accuracy 0.708 and weighted F1 0.758, and the top predicted cell types agree with anatomical landmarks in human colorectal cancer and mouse small intestine tissues.
abstract_sketch.impact: ENACT turns Visium HD bin-by-gene matrices into curated, spatially-resolved cell-type maps, enabling downstream SquidPy workflows (neighborhood enrichment, Moran's I, co-occurrence) at scale and producing paired image-plus-gene datasets for future vision-based cell classifiers.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Spatial transcriptomics enables gene expression mapping within tissue context; two families: sequencing-based (Visium, GeoMX) vs imaging-based (Xenium, MERFISH, CosMX).
Sequencing-based methods capture the whole transcriptome, splice variants, and support RNA-velocity, but traditional Visium has multi-cell spots.
Visium HD (10X Genomics) collects 2 micron by 2 micron bins; user-level 8 micron aggregation approximately matches cell size but rarely aligns to a single cell.
Problem: small or tightly spaced cells cause aggregated spots to overlap multiple cells and vice versa, causing contamination and mis-assignment.
Prior work (Bin2cell) uses Stardist for segmentation then expanded outlines to assign 2 micron bins by centroid, but struggles when cells are tightly spaced and a bin overlaps multiple cells.
ENACT contribution: comprehensive pipeline with cell segmentation, four bin-to-cell assignment strategies, three cell-type annotation engines, and SquidPy-ready outputs.

subsections.title: Cell Segmentation
content_bullets: Stardist UNet-based segmentation on full-resolution histology image; known to handle densely packed cells.

subsections.title: Bin-to-Cell Assignment
content_bullets: Represent bins and cell outlines as Shapely polygons; compute intersections via spatial join.
Discard bins intersecting no cell.
Four strategies: Naive (unique-bin only), plus three weight-based methods.

subsections.title: Cell Type Annotation
content_bullets: Sargent (score-based, marker-driven), CellAssign (probabilistic, marker-driven), CellTypist (logistic regression, pretrained).
Marker sources: PanglaoDB, Cell-Marker, and expert curation.

subsections.title: Downstream Analysis
content_bullets: Output AnnData objects consumable by SquidPy.
Enables neighborhood enrichment, Moran's I, co-occurrence; visualization via Tissuumaps.

subsections.title: Evaluation Datasets
content_bullets: Two synthetic Visium HD-like datasets built from Xenium and seqFISH+.
Pathologist annotations: anatomical landmarks plus manually labelled cell types.

subsections.title: Evaluating Bin-to-Cell Assignment Methods
content_bullets: Xenium synthetic, whole cell: Weight-By-Area best F1; Naive best precision but low recall because 25% of bins overlap more than one cell.
Nuclei-only: gap between Naive and weighted methods narrows; all methods have lower precision than whole-cell.
seqFISH+ synthetic: all four methods reach precision close to 1 and recall close to 0.99; only about 5% of bins overlap more than one cell; Naive recommended for speed.

subsections.title: Cell-Type Annotation Against Pathologist Labels
content_bullets: 20,991 cells annotated by pathologists (Epithelial/Stromal/Immune).
Four bin-to-cell x three annotation methods (12 combinations).
Weighted methods beat Naive across all annotators.
Sargent best; CellTypist second; CellAssign last.
Sargent + Weighted-by-Area: accuracy 0.708, weighted F1 0.758.
Confusion-matrix pattern: CellAssign misclassifies immune as epithelial; CellTypist confuses immune and epithelial; Sargent separates them well.

subsections.title: Cell-Type Enrichment in Anatomical Landmarks
content_bullets: Human colorectal cancer: muscular -> Smooth Muscle, B cells, Fibroblasts; normal epithelial -> Goblet, B cells, Crypt; tumoral epithelium -> Enterocytes, Goblet, B cells; stromal -> Fibroblasts, B cells, Endothelial.
Mouse small intestine: muscular -> Smooth muscle + Paneth; normal epithelium -> Paneth, Goblet, B cells; lymphoid -> B cells.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: First end-to-end tissue-agnostic pipeline for Visium HD.
Weighted methods outperform Naive on overlap-heavy tissues; Weighted-by-Area with Sargent is the recommended default.
Pipeline yields SquidPy-ready AnnData, enabling statistical spatial analyses.
Output pairs imaging and gene expression, supporting future training of vision-based cell classifiers.
Limitations: pathologist landmarks may contain mixed cell populations; marker-driven methods depend on curated marker lists; manual labels can include noise.
